title: pi-project schema and naming conventions — current state audit
question: What conventions does pi-project actually use for schemas, naming, lifecycle enums, cross-references, authorship, and hierarchy — and where do those conventions fall short of canonical engineering vocabulary and the L1–L5 artifact-ownership model?
findings_summary:
pi-project uses JSON Schema draft-07 with AJV validation, kebab-case file naming, and validate-on-write at the API boundary — all canonical. Every block is a JSON object with one required wrapper property whose value is an array (except architecture.schema.json which has multiple arrays). Items have required string id fields, free-form (no format constraint). State enums are per-block with no global lifecycle vocabulary. Cross-references are enforced by validateProject() in code (11 dimensional checks), not by $ref in schemas. 18 schemas exist as of snapshot date.

Six framework gaps surfaced: (1) No hierarchical/nested blocks — flat arrays only, no tasks/{id}.json or epic→story→task tree. (2) No per-scope finding registries — all findings land in global issues.json regardless of scope. (3) No materialized views — projectState() aggregates in-memory but produces no persisted, addressable, schema-validated derived block. (4) No authorship attestation at write time — only issues.schema.json has a source enum; block-api.ts does not stamp writes. (5) No state-machine validation on enum transitions — AJV validates current value membership but cannot express allowed transition paths. (6) No schema versioning or evolution — no version field, no $id, no $ref composition, no migration story. These six gaps became FGAP-001 through FGAP-006 and issues 055–060.
stale_conditions: .project/schemas/ contents change (schema count, field shapes, naming conventions)
packages/pi-project/src/block-api.ts adds authorship stamping or transition validation
packages/pi-project/src/project-sdk.ts validateProject() check count changes from 11
